lines.text: Chapter three, section one. What made synthetic biology possible.
Synthetic biology did not spring into existence as a clean engineering discipline by itself.
It emerged from the convergence, over roughly the last two decades of the twentieth century, of several distinct technological revolutions in the biosciences.

When the right combination of capabilities arrived — being able to read and write DNA cheaply, to share data globally, and to model cellular function quantitatively — synthetic biology became possible in a way it simply had not been before.
This chapter takes a tour of those enabling technologies.
The authors split the discussion into three parts.

First, they describe the enabling technologies that precipitated the rise of synthetic biology — the breakthroughs from molecular biology, genomics, and bioinformatics that made the whole enterprise feasible.

Second, they describe the foundations — the conscious engineering choices the synthetic biology community has made to give itself the kind of standard infrastructure that other engineering disciplines take for granted.
Third, they look ahead at the upcoming technologies that are likely to define the next generation of the field.
Before we begin, it is worth situating synthetic biology against its scientific neighbours.
Microbiology, one of the oldest disciplines in bioscience, studies single-celled organisms — bacteria, yeasts, and the like — which together form the most common chassis cells in synthetic biology.
Genetics looks at how cellular function and phenotypic differences between organisms link to specific genes and mutations.
Biochemistry connects biology and chemistry, revealing the molecular structures and chemistries — particularly of proteins like enzymes — that underlie cellular function.
Molecular biology completes the loop, identifying the molecules and processes inside cells that translate genetic information into biochemical activity.

And it is molecular biology, more than any of the others, that supplies the toolkit for engineering biology — the proteins, the cutting and rearranging enzymes, and the analytical methods used to sequence and synthesise DNA.

Synthetic biology can be understood as the moment when these four subjects, together with engineering and computer science, fused into something coherent — a discipline that does not merely describe biology but redesigns it.
